observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, (a) at the end of every month, reconcile the cashbook balances with the bank balances and accounting records; (b) ensure the correctness and accuracy of entries recorded in the Cashbooks; and (c) make the necessary adjustments in the cashbooks.

Further, we recommended that Staff from the OIC-Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to (a) closely coordinate and regularly reconcile their respective records for the early detection and correction of errors; and (b) exert more efforts and conduct thorough analysis of the entries in the cashbook and investigate the cause of the unreconciled differences.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Inventory Committee to take the following actions: (a) carry out a thorough physical inventory count of all PPE owned by the Municipality, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020; and (b) strengthen efforts to acquire the necessary documentation or information for the proper recognition and valuation of donated PPEs, or alternatively, ensure they are recorded in compliance with the guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the:
(a) OIC-General Services Officer and OIC-Municipal Accountant reconcile accounting records with the RPCPPE to resolve the deficiencies and discrepancies in the PPE account balances and determine whether the case qualifies for the application of a one-time cleansing of PPE account balances in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020; (b) department heads to account all donated PPEs under their custody and submit all relevant documents to the Municipal Accounting Office for the accurate recognition in the books; (c) OIC-Municipal Accountant to (i) recognize the donated PPEs in the books of accounts; and (ii) make the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct balances of the PPE account; and (d) OIC-Municipal Accountant, together with the OIC-General Services Officer and OIC-Municipal Engineer, to initiate the proper recognition and inventory of the Local Road Network System and the drafting of the Local Road Network Map of the Municipality.

Moreover, we also recommended that the Local Chief Executive form a committee to locate and validate the 496 parcels of land and oversee the titling process to register them in the Municipality's name, ensuring proper asset management and legal recognition.
observations.managementAction: 4.26. The concerned municipal offices committed to work collaboratively to address the noted observations and ensure full compliance with the recommendations.
observations.reason: Incomplete physical inventory due to lack of personnel.

observations.recommendation:
1.9. We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer (a) perform monthly
reconciliation of the cashbook balances with the bank statements and accounting
records; (b) verify the accuracy and completeness of all entries recorded in the
cashbooks; and (c) promptly make any necessary adjustments to correct
discrepancies identified in the cashbooks.

1.10. Further, we recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant and Municipal
Treasurer (a) sustain close coordination and undertake regular reconciliations of
their respective records to ensure the prompt detection and rectification of
discrepancies; and (b) diligently conduct comprehensive analyses of cashbook entries
and thoroughly investigate the underlying causes of any unreconciled variances.

2.11. We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to, in coordination
with the Municipal Assessor, furnish the OIC-Municipal Accountant with a duly
certified list of taxpayers showing the names and the amount due and collectible based
on RPT Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card to provide an accurate and reliable
basis for the setting up of RPT and SET receivables at the beginning of the year for
fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.

2.12. Further, we recommended and the OIC-Municipal Accountant agreed to
reconcile the RPT and SET Receivables Subsidiary Ledgers with the reported
delinquent real property taxes of the MTO so that the necessary adjustment(s) in the
books of accounts can be recorded to establish the correct balances of the accounts.

3.13. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) direct the Inventory
Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all inventory items every six
months to verify account balances and submit a complete and accurate RPCI; and
(b) establish policies and procedures for inventory control and monitoring; (c) ensure
the timely preparation and submission of inventory issuance reports; and (d) strictly
comply with the NGAS Manual on the proper recording, valuation, and reporting of
inventories.

4.23. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Inventory
Committee to take the following actions: (a) carry out a thorough physical inventory
count of all PPE owned by the Municipality, in accordance with COA Circular No.
2020-006 dated January 31, 2020; and (b) strengthen efforts to acquire the necessary
documentation or information for the proper recognition and valuation of donated
PPEs, or alternatively, ensure they are recorded in compliance with the guidelines set
forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006.

4.24. We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the:
(a) OIC-General Services Officer and OIC-Municipal Accountant reconcile
accounting records with the RPCPPE to resolve the deficiencies and
discrepancies in the PPE account balances and determine whether the case
qualifies for the application of a one-time cleansing of PPE account balances
in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020;
(b) department heads to account all donated PPEs under their custody and
submit all relevant documents to the Municipal Accounting Office for the
accurate recognition in the books;
(c) OIC-Municipal Accountant to (i) recognize the donated PPEs in the books of
accounts; and (ii) make the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct
balances of the PPE account; and
(d) OIC-Municipal Accountant, together with the OIC-General Services Officer
and OIC-Municipal Engineer, to initiate the proper recognition and
inventory of the Local Road Network System and the drafting of the Local
Road Network Map of the Municipality.

4.25. Moreover, we also recommended that the Local Chief Executive form a
committee to locate and validate the 496 parcels of land and oversee the titling process
to register them in the Municipality's name, ensuring proper asset management and
legal recognition.

5.13. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the OIC-Municipal
Accountant to (a) make the necessary adjusting entry for the affected accounts; (b)
guarantee that all adjustments should be properly supported by adequate
documentation and the disclosure of Prior Period Adjustments in the Notes to the
Financial Statements in compliance with the requirements of IPSAS; and (c) ensure
that internal controls and procedures should be implemented to prevent similar
issues in the future, including the training of personnel on the proper recording,
valuation, and documentation of PPAs.

6.13. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
(a) OIC-Municipal Accountant to (i) trace the unidentified beginning balances of
the CIP account and review the relevant records or documents to verify their
validity and make necessary adjustments; (ii) coordinate with the Municipal
Engineering Office to determine the current status of projects recorded under the
CIP account; (iii) regularly consult with the MEO to identify all projects that
should be recorded under the CIP account, ensuring accurate and fair presentation
in the financial statements; and (iv) make the appropriate adjusting entries to
reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts.
(b) OIC-Municipal Engineer to (i) conduct a year-end inspection of all ongoing
projects and require contractors to submit progress billings along with all
necessary supporting documents, to ensure the accurate recording of completed
works in the Municipality’s financial records; and (ii) accurately record and
validate the status of all municipal projects in the RGPPAs as of the reporting date,
to ensure timely and reliable reporting.

7.14. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as the Chairman of the
Local Development Council (LDC), direct the members and secretariat of the LDC,
along with the implementing departments, to (a) strengthen their collaboration to
better align the budget with local development plans and improve project execution;
and (b) conduct thorough planning and extensive research to consider all factors that
might affect the implementation and timelines of the planned PPAs to minimize, if
not avoid, any event that can cause delays in the implementation of the PPAs to ensure
the prompt realization of benefits for the intended beneficiaries.

8.21. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the MDRRM
Officer to (a) create a comprehensive annual plan for the LDRRMF that ensures a
more balanced allocation of funds throughout the year, prioritizing preparedness
activities to reduce the reliance on reactive relief efforts; (b) prepare the complete
LDRRMF Investment Plan for current year’s appropriation and prior years’
unexpended balance; (c) furnish the Audit Team, OCD and DILG-LGOO with the
copy of the LDRRMFIP and monthly utilization reports in accordance with the
requirements of COA Circular Nos. 2012-002 and 2014-002; (d) monitor and ensure
the implementation of the DRRM-related PPAs.

9.6. We recommended that the Municipality strengthen and continue its adherence
to the provisions of COA Circular No. 94-013, along with other applicable
government accounting and auditing standards, to ensure that all future fund
transfers are properly documented, efficiently utilized for their intended purposes,
and accurately reported.

9.7. We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct all
implementing departments to continue monitoring the implementation of programs
and projects to ensure the timely implementation/completion thereof.

10.20. We recommended and the Bids and Awards Committee agreed to (a) ensure
that all procurement activities fully comply with the provisions of the revised IRR of
RA No. 9184 and COA Circular 2012-001; (b) prepare and secure approval of the
ABC before initiating any procurement activity; (c) mandate that all changes,
modifications, or clarifications in the bidding documents be issued through duly
approved Supplemental Bid Bulletins and provide a written justification for the
identified modification; (d) ensure that bid documents for infrastructure projects
include complete technical specifications to establish clear performance and quality
standards and promote transparency and fairness in the procurement process; and
(e) enforce that no NTP is issued unless the COSH Program has been submitted, in
accordance with DOLE and procurement rules.

10.21. Further, we recommended and the OIC-Municipal Accountant agreed to, in
coordination with the concerned municipal offices or units, (a) submit all lacking or
incomplete supporting documents related to disbursements made to contractors, to
establish the validity, legality, and regularity of the transactions; and
(b) going forward, warrant that all payments to contractors are fully supported with
complete and appropriate documentation, in accordance with applicable accounting,
auditing, and procurement rules and regulations.

11.8. We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive, instruct
the Municipal Engineering Office to (a) exercise strict oversight in the
implementation of all infrastructure projects to ensure adherence to approved plans
and specifications; (b) conduct regular inspections and monitoring of completed
projects to facilitate the early identification and resolution of any structural defects;
and (c) hold the concerned contractor in charge by requiring the prompt rectification
of any defective work, at no additional cost to the Municipality, in accordance with
the provisions of the contract.

12.13. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, in his capacity as
Chairperson of the GFPS issue clear directives to all GFPS members, including the
GAD Technical Working Group (TWG), to sustain and further strengthen their
compliance with the prescribed requirements for the institutionalization of GAD
mechanisms. This includes the continued implementation of strategies that promote a
gender-responsive and inclusive environment within the local government.

13.10. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, in his capacity as Chairman
of the LSB, (a) formally designate a specific member of the LSB to take responsibility
for compliance with the requirements under Item 6.1 of Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017,
and ensure that the SEF Utilization Reports are regularly and timely prepared and
posted in all required platforms and conspicuous public places, including the
municipal bulletin board, official website, and other appropriate communication
channels.; and (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to establish regular
coordination with the Municipal Budget Office to ensure that all SEF Utilization
Reports are accurate and properly reconciled in accordance with applicable
guidelines.

14.22. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Human
Resource Management Officer to (a) conduct a thorough review and evaluation of the
Municipality’s existing organizational structure to properly identify and establish the
appropriate human resource complement; (b) consider appointing qualified JO
personnel to fill vacant regular positions; (c) ensure that the hiring of JO personnel is
based on necessity of services, as well as their qualifications and performance; (d)
verify that the rates indicated in the payroll match those specified in the respective
contracts; (e) review the accomplishment reports submitted by JO personnel ,
ensuring that the reported outputs are measurable and detailed, with adequate
documentation to support the services rendered; and (f) require the unverified 84 JO
personnel to appear at the COA office for their evaluation and verification.

15.9. We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) instruct the
Municipal Treasurer, together with other concerned municipal offices, to strategize
and exert more efforts to enforce the collections of delinquent real property taxes
which includes the conduct of intensive tax campaigns and sending of Notices of
Delinquencies; (b) strengthen the monitoring of taxpayer accounts and historical
payment trends to identify habitual delinquencies and strategize appropriate
interventions; and (c) initiate the enforcement of legal remedies pursuant to the
pertinent provisions of RA No. 7160 in order to persuade the taxpayers to pay their
taxes on time.

16.21. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the MTO to (a) levy,
assess and collect business taxes in compliance with the Municipality’s Revenue Code,
ensuring proper interpretation and compliance with its provisions and to avoid the
future miscomputations; (b) require the submission of sworn statement of gross
receipts or sales and certified copies of income tax returns so as to come-up with an
accurate basis for the assessment of business taxes; (c) conduct periodic re-
assessments of registered businesses; and (d) for the over/under-collection of business
taxes – (i) issue supplemental assessment and pursue collection of the tax
deficiencies for the recovery of under-collections identified during the audit; and
(ii) for the over-collection of business taxes - review the accounts of businesses
identified as having overpaid taxes and if possible, issue refunds or applying tax
credits toward future tax obligations, in accordance with the provisions of the
Municipal Revenue Code and applicable national laws.

17.8. We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive instruct
the Bids and Awards Committee to (a) submit the unsubmitted contracts mentioned
above; (b) furnish the Audit Team with the copy of the purchase orders and contract
documents not later than five working days from the issuance of the purchase
orders and perfection of contracts together with duly accomplished supporting
documents; and (c) establish and enforce stricter internal controls to ensure the
timely submission of contracts and purchase orders (POs), as required by COA
Circular No. 2009-001.

18.9. However, we reiterated our recommendation that the Accounting
Department, Human Resource Management Office, and Municipal
Treasurer’s Office strengthen their coordination and internal controls by (a) conducting
regular monthly reconciliations between the recognized collections of contributions,
premiums, and loan amortization payments, and the corresponding remittances
made to the respective agencies; and (b) identifying and implementing the necessary
adjustments to address the discrepancies noted between payroll records and general
ledger balances.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
(a) OIC-Municipal Accountant to (i) trace the unidentified beginning balances of the CIP account and review the relevant records or documents to verify their validity and make necessary adjustments; (ii) coordinate with the Municipal Engineering Office to determine the current status of projects recorded under the CIP account; (iii) regularly consult with the MEO to identify all projects that should be recorded under the CIP account, ensuring accurate and fair presentation in the financial statements; and (iv) make the appropriate adjusting entries to reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts.
(b) OIC-Municipal Engineer to (i) conduct a year-end inspection of all ongoing projects and require the contractors to submit progress billings along with all necessary supporting documents, to ensure the accurate recording of completed works in the Municipality’s financial records; and (ii) accurately record and validate the status of all municipal projects in the RGPPAs as of the reporting date, to ensure timely and reliable reporting.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Inventory Committee to (a) conduct a comprehensive physical inventory count covering all the PPEs owned by the Municipality pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020; (b) intensify efforts to obtain the needed documents or information for the recognition and valuation of the donated PPEs, otherwise record them in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006; and (c) coordinate with the Municipal Accounting Office for the reconciliation of accounting records with the RPCPPE to address the identified deficiencies and variances in the PPE account.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) OIC-General Services Officer and OIC-Municipal Accountant, after exhaustion of all diligent efforts, to evaluate the PPE discrepancies if the case would qualify for the application of one-time cleansing of PPE account balances for the derecognition of non-existing/missing PPE items under COA Circular No. 2020-006, provided that all the requirements specified in Items 7 and 8 are met; (b) department heads to account all donated PPEs under their custody and submit all pertinent documents to the Municipal Accounting Office for the accurate recognition in the books; (c) OIC-Municipal Accountant to (i) recognize the donated PPEs in the books of accounts; and (ii) make the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct balances of the PPE account; and (d) OIC-Municipal Accountant, together with the OIC-General Services Officer and OIC-Municipal Engineer, to initiate the proper recognition and inventory of the Local Road Network System and the drafting of the Local Road Network Map of the Municipality.
Moreover, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive create a dedicated committee that will locate the 496 parcels of land and process the titling of the parcels of land, if confirmed owned by the Municipality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to (a) trace the unidentified beginning balances of the CIP account, and verify the records or pertinent documents to establish their validity so that necessary adjustments could be made; (b) coordinate with the Municipal Engineering Office on the status of the projects recorded under the CIP account; (c) regularly verify from the Municipal Engineering Office the status of all projects that needs to be recorded under the CIP account to fairly present the same in the financial statements; and (d) make the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts.

Further we recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Engineer to (a) conduct an inspection of all ongoing projects at year-end and instruct the contractors to submit progress billing, together with other necessary documents, for the proper recording of the completed works in the Municipality’s books of accounts; and (b) properly record and validate the status of all projects of the Municipality in the Report on Government Projects/Programs/Activities as of reporting date.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive instructed the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to, henceforth, (a) stop the practice of transferring funds in advance into the STF account; and (b) coordinate with the Municipal Budget Office and Municipal Accountant’s Office for the correct amount of unexpended/unobligated QRF and DRRMF-MOOE that should be transferred to the STF account.

Further, we recommended and the Local Chief Executive instructed the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to return the excess amount transferred to the General Fund bank account.

Moreover, we recommended and the OIC-Municipal Accountant coordinated with the Municipal Treasurer for the (a) necessary transfer of the remaining balance of unexpended/unobligated CY 2020 and March 2021 LDRRMF totaling Ᵽ267,961.82; and (b) made the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct balance of the affected accounts.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as the Chairman of the Local Development Council, direct the members and secretariat of the LDC, along with the implementing departments, to (a) continue enhancing the process of planning, executing, monitoring, and evaluating programs and projects prioritized under the 20% Development Fund to guarantee that the programs and projects are well-prepared and ready for implementation; and (b) aim for the timely completion of the projects through collective efforts to ensure the prompt realization of benefits for the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct all implementing departments to continue monitoring the implementation of programs and projects to ensure the timely implementation/completion thereof.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive instructed the MDRRM Officer to (a) prepare the LDRRMF Investment Plan for current year’s appropriation and prior years’ unexpended balance; (b) furnish the Audit Team, OCD and DILG-LGOO with the copy of the LDRRMFIP and monthly utilization reports in accordance with the requirements of COA Circular Nos. 2012-002 and 2014-002; (c) monitor and ensure the implementation of the DRRM-related PPAs.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, through the Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Office (MENRO), (a) in coordination with school and barangays, educate the public through the conduct of continuous forums and seminars to raise awareness and increase the compliance rate on segregation at source; (b) devise ways to improve the system of collection and disposal of garbage and wastes; (c) firmly impose fines and penalties against violators of prohibited acts to improve compliance rate and to provide sufficient funding for solid waste management activities; and (d) develop the MRF and provide the necessary equipment to improve its operational capability.

Further, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) MENRO and Municipal Administrator to generate policy that will cover the systematic flow of receipt and issuance of the cement from RCBM which include the personnel in charge of receipt, issuance and safekeeping, and the documents and reports that needed to be prepared and forwarded to the Accounting Department for  recording in the books; and (b) OIC-Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the MENRO and Municipal Administrator properly account and record all the succeeding receipt and utilization of the cement given by the Republic Cement & Building Materials, Inc.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as chairman of the GFPS, instruct the members and the GAD Technical Working Group (TWG) to (a) continue the GFPS’ compliance with the requirements on the institutionalization of GAD mechanisms and mandate of promoting a gender-responsive environment in the local government; and (b) in coordination with other municipal offices, to ensure that the GAD Plan and Budget and the submitted and maintained GAD reports and records are properly prepared and reconciled.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive, instruct the Municipal Engineering Office to (a) closely monitor the implementation of all projects; (b) to regularly check the condition of completed projects for early detection of defects; and (c) require the Contractor concerned to perform the appropriate repair of the portion of the projects found to be defective at no additional cost to the Municipality.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Bids and Awards Committee to (a) submit the unsubmitted contracts; and (b) furnish the Audit Team with copy of the purchase orders and contract documents not later than five working days from the issuance of the purchase orders and perfections of contracts together with duly accomplished supporting documents.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) (a) strictly observe the provisions of updated revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and COA Circular 2012-001 on the process/procedures in the receipt and opening of bids, evaluation of bids, post-qualification of the lowest calculated bid, preparation of contract and issuance of Notice to Proceed and completeness of the documents supporting the procurement activities, contract and disbursement vouchers; and (b) submit a written explanation on the noted inconsistencies with the provisions of the Revised IRR .

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with other municipal offices/units, (a) submit the lacking documents noted; and (b) henceforth, ensure that all payments to contractors are properly supported with the required documents.

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Engineer (a) remind the contractors to cause the implementation of the necessary preventive measures such as the provision of PPE and imposition of wearing the same during project implementation to eliminate or minimize the exposure of their workers to hazard; and (b) observe the provisions of the updated revised IRR on the appropriate period of accepting the completed projects to maximize the time that the projects are covered with warranty.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) Business Permits and Licensing Unit (BPLU) to (i) levy and collect taxes in compliance with the Municipality’s Revenue Code; and (ii) apply the appropriate imposition of business tax for newly started businesses; (b) Municipal Treasurer's Office (MTO) and other concerned offices to revise the Revenue Code in order to clearly express the intention of the Municipal Government to arrive at higher taxes; (c) BPLU and MTO to require the submission of sworn statement of gross receipts or sales and certified copies of income tax returns so as to come-up with an accurate basis for the computation of business taxes; (d) BPLU and MTO to verify, assess and validate the noted under collection of ₱510,758.49 and consequently demand the collection/payment thereof; and (e) the concerned municipal officials and employees to submit a written explanation on the noted deficiencies.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Accounting Department, Human Resource Management Office, and Municipal Treasurer’s Office (a) conduct monthly reconciliation between the recognized collected contributions/premiums and loan amortization payments and the remittances made; and (b) conduct thorough evaluation of the affected accounts to determine the noted unremitted balances.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive
(a) create an Inventory Committee to facilitate the conduct of the physical count of all inventory items semestrally to establish the correct balances for each account and submit a complete and accurate Report on the Physical Count of Inventory (RPCI), as required; and
(b) direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant and Head of the General Services Unit to continue accomplishing its complete supplies ledger cards and property cards, respectively, for all inventories.

Further, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all department heads and designated supply officers to
(a) formulate policies and procedures in the control and monitoring of all inventories purchased and issued;
(b) ensure the preparation and timely submission of reports pertaining to the issuance of inventory items; and
(c) strictly comply with the rules and regulations contained in the NGAS Manual for the proper recording, valuation and preparation of the required inventory reports.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, instruct the Municipal Health Office to (a) coordinate with the Provincial Government and DOH RO III for the noted deficiencies on the transferred vaccines; (b) prepare and submit to the Audit Team a detailed record of received and issued vaccines; and (c) henceforth, ensure the preparation and maintenance of complete and accurate records and reports.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Officer conduct thorough review of the masterlist of vaccinated individuals and make the necessary corrections.

We recommended that the (a) Municipal Treasurer make the necessary adjustments in the cashbooks; and (b) Municipal Accountant draw the necessary journal entry to reflect the correct balance of the cash accounts.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant closely coordinate and regularly reconcile their respective records for the early detection and correction of errors.

We recommended that the Municipal officials and employees observe strictly the guidelines on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances, and effect the payment of expenses through check payable directly to the supplier or creditor except in cases where (a) needed items are extremely necessary and urgent which cannot be conveniently paid by check, and (b) the item/service is petty in nature.

Further, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) demand the immediate liquidation or refund of the outstanding year-end balances of Petty Cash Fund; (b) instruct the concerned accountable personnel to liquidate their cash advances as soon as the purposes were served, and no additional cash advances shall be granted unless the previous ones were liquidated or settled; and (c) require explanation on the delayed liquidation of the funds.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) instruct the Inventory Committee to facilitate the conduct of the physical count of all inventory items semestrally to establish the correct balances for each account and submit a complete and accurate Report on the Physical Count of Inventory; and (b) direct the Municipal Accountant and Head of the General Services Unit to prepare and maintain complete supplies ledger cards and property cards for all inventories.

Further we recommended that Local Chief Executive direct all department heads and designated supply officers to (a) formulate policies and procedures in the control and monitoring of all inventories purchased and received; (b) ensure the preparation and timely submission of reports pertaining to the issuance of inventory items; and (c) strictly comply with the rules and regulations contained in the NGAS Manual for the proper recording, valuation and preparation of the required inventory reports.

Moreover, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct officials and employees concerned to ensure that (a) all disbursements were supported with complete documentation; and (b) payment for goods and services be made through check, directly payable to suppliers, except in cases where needed items are extremely necessary and urgent which cannot be conveniently paid by check.

We reiterated our recommendations that the (a) Head of the General Service Unit and Municipal Accountant (i) maintain complete property and ledger cards; and (ii) exert more efforts in reconciling the reported PPE items in the inventory report and accounting records; and (b) Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the General Services Officer, determine all small value items for reclassification and make the necessary adjusting entry to reflect the correct balances of the PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) conduct thorough review of the liabilities accounts to ensure that only valid obligations of the Municipality are reflected in the financial statements; (b) prepare the required ledgers to provide the necessary information; (c) ensure that all claims shall be supported with complete documentations; (d) prepare a Schedule of Accounts Payable which includes all the necessary information such as name and address of the creditor, date the obligation was incurred and aging of the account; (e) revert if any, the excess payables and those which had been outstanding for more than two years and without actual claims, administrative or judicial, or which were not covered by perfected contracts or records.

We recommended that the Local Development Council, chaired by the Local Chief Executive, strengthen the cycle of planning, execution, monitoring and evaluation of programs and projects for prioritization under the 20% DF in order to ensure well prepared and implementation-ready programs and projects.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Development Council and Bids and Awards Committee, in coordination with the Municipal Engineering Office, include in the AIP and APP the specific period of implementation of the PPAs for the ensuing year to ensure timely implementation thereof.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Engineer and other implementing departments to monitor the implementation of programs and projects to ensure timely completion and avoid non-compliance with the conditions of the transfers and lessen the eventual termination of projects and return of funds.

Further, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to return the unutilized funds from the NDRRMF to the National Treasury.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, instruct the Municipal Engineer’s Office to (a) closely monitor the implementation of all projects; (b) create monitoring team that will regularly check the condition of completed projects for early detection of defects; and (c) require the Contractor concerned to cause the repair of the portion of the projects found to be defective at no additional cost to the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (a) prepare a comprehensive and an in-depth LDRRMP, both for current and previous years’ LDRRMF; and (b) conduct thorough evaluation and planning of the DRRM PPAs for effective and efficient utilization of the LDRRMF.

Moreover, we recommended that the LDRRM Officer and Municipal Accountant (a) ensure that all DRRMF are included on the Report on Utilization of DRRMF for easy monitoring of the utilization of the fund; and (b) prepare all required reports pursuant to COA Circular Nos. 2012-002 and 2014-002.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant recognize the appropriate expense accounts for the recording of expenditures charged under the unexpended LDRRMF.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal GAD Focal Point System to monitor the implementation of GAD-related PPAS to ensure that gender issues and/or gaps faced by its constituents and employees are properly addressed.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as Chairman of the LSB, the Co-Chairman and other members of the LSB (a) conduct consultation/discussion with heads of schools and other stakeholders for strategic prioritization in the allocation of the SEF and to properly plan the PPAs to be implemented from the fund; and (b) coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Budget Officer and Acting Municipal Accountant for the available SEF and maximize the utilization of the Fund to attain its goals and objectives.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, instruct the concerned municipal offices to (a) stop paying financial assistance through petty cash fund; (b) henceforth, strictly comply with the rules and guidelines in the grant of financial assistance particularly on the required supporting documents; and (c) submit an explanation on the inconsistencies noted between the recorded assistance and those actually received by the beneficiaries.

Further, we recommended that elected officials refrain from the practice of granting financial assistance directly to its constituents.

Moreover, we recommended that Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer, Municipal Treasurer, and Municipal Accountant to devise a coordinated system in the assessment, evaluation, and payment of financial assistance to ensure that all the beneficiaries are eligible, the transactions are properly documented, and the assistance are actually received by the beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Management hire job order and contract of service personnel based on necessity and perform periodic performance evaluation and assessment to ensure value for money and avoid wastage of government funds.

Also, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive, instruct the Human Resource Management Officer to (a) submit the signed contracts, with clearly defined duties and responsibilities of the JO and COS personnel hired in CY 2022; (b) attach to the payroll the DTRs and Accomplishment Reports of its JO and COS personnel to support payment of wages; and (c) refer to the provisions of Section 2, Rule XI of the Revised Omnibus Rules on Appointments and Other Personnel Actions and CSC Memorandum Circular No. 24, series of 2002 regarding the policy guidelines on the hiring, supervision, and payment of JO personnel.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) strictly observe the provisions of RA No. 9184 specially on (a) the completeness of the documents that should document the procurement activities and support the contract; (b) evaluation of bidding documents; and (c) the posting of procurement activities with the required media and within the prescribed period.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Engineer require the contractors to prepare the SWA.

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) deduct the 10% retention money from each progress payment for infrastructure projects pursuant to Item 6.1 of Annex E of the revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and (b) in coordination with the Municipal Engineering Office, ensure that all payments to contractors are properly supported with the required documents.

We recommended that Local Chief Executive, instruct the Bids and Awards Committee and the Head of the General Services Unit to (a) submit the unsubmitted contracts and POs for evaluation and review; and (b) furnish the Audit Team with copy of the purchase orders and contract documents not later than five working days from the issuance of the purchase orders and perfections of contracts together with duly accomplished supporting documents.

We recommended that the Management strictly comply with the pertinent laws, rules, and regulations on the timely and full remittance of premiums/contributions and loan amortization to avoid payment of penalties and interest.

In addition, we also recommended that the Accounting Department, Human Resource Management Office, and Municipal Treasurer’s Office conduct monthly reconciliation between the recognized collected contributions/premiums and loan amortization payments and the remittances made.

Further, we recommended that Accounting Department evaluate the prior year’s unremitted balances of the Due to BIR, HDMF and PhilHealth as well as the negative balance of Due to GSIS account to determine if these are valid obligations of the Municipality and immediately remit or adjust the same.

We recommended that the Management 

submit (i) a satisfactory explanation on resorting to payment through cash advance of the various foregoing disbursements not considered as emergency in nature and did not fall under the categories of the two types of cash advances  as per Circular COA Circular No. 97-002, (ii) the lacking documents as in the remarks column of the matrix in Annex A, and Item 1.7, and (iii) legal basis on the disbursements considered as personal expenses, and honorarium of P24,000.00 as in Item 1.9, otherwise, refund the same amount;
observe strictly the guidelines on the granting and liquidations of cash advances; and

We recommended that the Management 

(a) submit a satisfactory explanation on the (i) mode of payments and piecemeal procurements, (ii) incurred expenses of meals over the allocations, and (iii) inappropriate expenses under the BGCM; 
(b) submit the required documents as mentioned in Items 1.9 and 1.11; and 

(c) refrain from incurring expenses other than the purpose for which the fund is intended, and more than the approved allocations.

We recommended that the Management continue being compliant with the provision on the regular remittance of withheld taxes within the due date, and ensure that withheld taxes during the month are remitted in full on the following month.

In 2017, the Municipality appointed the designated MDRRM Officer for the position of Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Office (LDRRMO) V but this was not attested by the Civil Service Commission since the position item was only appropriate for provincial and city government. In 2018,  SB ordinance was enacted for the change of position item from LDRRMO V to Municipal Government Department Head, which is for the approval of the Sanggunian Panlalawigan.  The designated MDRRM Officer is supported by three regular staff, two of which are currently assigned at the Office of the Municipal Planning and Development Office, and at the same time assisted in the Research and Planning of the LDRRM Office.

The MDRRMO designate being new in the post, and the MDRRMC having no training related to ICS, the LGU is in the process of collaborating with the Office of the Civil Defense and Provincial Disaster Risk Reduction Management Office to materialize the plan of conducting ICS Development Course for the MDRRMC and CBDRRMC members for the organization of a concrete ICS structure to strengthen a proper and systematic response capacity.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Members of the Municipal Local School Board:
a) 	prepare a balanced budget with conscious and concerted efforts of observing compliance with the legal and regulatory requirements on SEF budgeting, allocation and utilization and refrain from accommodating expenditures for activities for which the DepEd has the direct mandate to execute; and

observations.managementAction:
This matter was brought to the attention of the Local School Board & LGU Officials concerned in the preparation of the 2016 Local School Board Budget and its proper utilization thereof.

In addition, realignment of funds was made in 2015 to conform to the existing rules.  The LSB Budget for 2017 was already programmed in accordance with R.A. 5447.
observations.reason: Some expenditures/ programs were already realized/utilized when the AOM for 2015 was released.

observations.recommendation: We commended management of its efforts to achieve effective planning, implementation and monitoring of the Agency’s Solid Waste Management plan, especially in their effort to search for alternative method in the reduction of residual wastes being hauled by the Metro Clark Corporation.  However, we recommended that the seven non-compliant barangays be enjoined to pass their respective ordinances on the mandatory segregation of waste, among other basic solid waste management regulations.

We commended management of its efforts to uplift the living condition of the poor.  We would still recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the:

a) 	Municipal Social Welfare Officer to ensure that the nutritional status of the minimal number of under-nourished children improve to normal and exert more efforts to maintain or improve further the large number of children within the normal weight; and

b) 	As far as practicable, considering the maintaining balance of the account, Municipal Treasurer to comply with Section 2.4 of DSWD Administrative Order No. 08 Series of 2012 on the opening of a separate bank account for the Program.
observations.managementAction: The LCE directed the MSWDO to comply.
observations.reason: Documentary requirements for the opening of a separate bank account are not yet available when the funds to be used for the purpose need to be downloaded already. Management considers this process impractical as many bank accounts are being maintained already.  However, separate records are kept by the Accounting Office for the receipt and disbursement of these funds.

We also recommended that management comply with the recommendation of the National Meat Inspection Service to produce clean, wholesome, healthy and sound meat for the consuming public, particularly the residents of Guagua, Pampanga to avoid  risking their health and safety.

observations.recommendation:
1.6    	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive
require the Inventory Committee  to conduct a complete physical inventory of PPE to determine the actual existence of all assets owned by the Municipality;
require the Property Officer and the Municipal Accountant (i) to reconcile the property and the accounting records of the PPE accounts balances with the physical inventory report; and
require the Municipal Treasurer or the  General Services  Officer to ascertain the status of parcels of land and buildings owned by the Municipality with titling issues and work for the titling thereof in the name of the Municipality of Guagua, assuming funds have been provided therefore.

2.11	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to (i) limit the granting of cash advances only to those provided and prescribed by the provisions, and (ii) comply strictly with the regulation that all payments for regular expenses must be made through checks payable directly to suppliers/sellers except for petty expenditures to avoid the opportunity for possible abuse or misuse of government funds; and (b) demand from the concerned official with unliquidated cash advance the immediate settlement of his accountabilities and require explanation on the delayed liquidation of the funds.

4.8	We recommended that the (a) Local Development Council continue to propose and allocate for high-impact capital expenditures in the utilization of the 20% Development Fund pursuant to the guidelines set out under Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 of the DILG and DBM to attain the developmental intent of the Fund; and (b) Management ensure that the funds transferred to the barangays are utilized in accordance with their intended purposes through close monitoring for the achievement of the objective of the program.

6.7	We recommended that the (a) approving and processing officials of the Municipality on disbursements  require the Federation to submit documents to substantiate the disbursements made on the transferred amount of P350,000.00 for the accounting thereof and for the audit of the transactions; and (b) officials involved in budget preparation and approval, henceforth, align or attribute all  programs/projects/activities (PPAs) for senior citizens within the Municipality’s  approved annual budget of which disbursements thereto shall be subject to the usual accounting and auditing rules and regulations instead of coursing the PPAs implementation thru fund transfers.

7.10   We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, the Municipal Development Council and the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board  (a) earmark the financial support of the Municipality to the barangays under the 20% Development Fund on Solid Waste Management Program for the establishment of MRFs and for the implementation of waste segregation at source;  (b) enforce strictly the no segregation, no collection policy and the no-littering policy and intensify information campaign among the residents; and (c) consider embarking on effective projects and programs to support the Municipality’s ecological solid waste management program utilizing the remaining portion of the  parcel of land housing the MRF.

8.14  We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD Focal Person to  comply with the provisions of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 and COA Circular No. 2014-001 particularly on the conduct of gender audit and gender mainstreaming in the Municipality. We recommended further that GAD Fund be allocated only on PPAs concerning gender issues.

9.5	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, along with the other offices/officials concerned, comply with the submission of the lacking documents/information as discussed above, for the evaluation of the Audit Team and subsequent audit action. Henceforth, the completeness of documents and such other data required pertinent to the transactions to substantiate the payments together with the disbursement vouchers be submitted promptly to the Auditor’s Office.

10.5  We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to (i) strictly comply with the regulation on the rendition of accounts in order not to delay the verification and audit of transactions; (ii) observe the due dates in the submission to the Audit Team of the required documents; and (iii) submit without delay the ninety seven (97) DVs amounting to ₱11,641,357.70 to avoid the issuance of Notice of Suspension.

11.4	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to invariably remit the taxes withheld within the prescribed period as provided in Revenue Memorandum Circular 23-2012.

12.9	We recommended that the Management comply with the provision of the Department of Budget and Management Local Budget Circular (LBC) No. 98 dated October 14, 2011 and Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs by (a) studying the present and organizational structure of the Municipality taking into consideration the service requirements; (b) making sure that excessive and overlapping functions with those of the permanent employees are eliminated or reduced; and (c) complying strictly with the 45% PS limitation as provided in Section 325 of RA 7160.

13.6 	We recommended that the (i) Management continue to adopt economy measures without sacrificing the delivery of basic services to constituents particularly to the most disadvantaged sector of the Municipality; and (ii) Municipal Accountant in coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Treasurer exert efforts to establish the accuracy and validity of the balances of the trust liability accounts for the determination of the actual obligations of the Municipality.

We recommended that the  Municipal Treasurer (a) update and initiate efforts in aiming a certain percentage of taxpayers annually, until all taxpayers are covered,  in the preparation of a certified list of taxpayers with the corresponding amount due and collectible for the year;  (b) furnish the same to the Municipal Accountant who shall use it as basis for the setting-up of RPT/SET Receivables at the beginning of the year; and (c) in coordination with the Assessor, send demand or collection letters to owners of real properties with long overdue accounts of more than ten (10) years for increased collection.
observations.managementAction: As of December 2015, the Municipal Treasurer’s Office already prepared the list of delinquent taxpayers for the RPT and the same was furnished to the Accounting Office which was used as their basis in setting up the RPT/Set Receivables at the beginning of 2016 in the Books of the Municipality.  Likewise, the matter was brought to the attention of the Assessor’s Office in cooperation with the Treasurer’s Office to send demand letters to owners of Real Properties with long overdue accounts to increase collection.  As per records of the Treasury Office, RPT collection for 2016 increased by 31% as compared to 2015.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the (a) Municipal Treasurer and other officials concerned to exert more efforts to locate the documents, such as deed of sale or donation, to establish ownership over the lands; (b) Municipal Accountant (i)  to coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer, General Service Officer and the Municipal Assessor for the establishment of the fair value of the lots owned by the Municipality for proper valuation in the books; and (ii) to maintain Real Property Ledger Cards once the ownership of the lots are established, and (c) if ownership over the land is established, the General Services Officer initiate efforts towards the titling of these real estate properties in favor of the Municipality.

The list will be submitted soon with all the information needed for ease of reference  (See attached letter of Municipal Assessor dated January 20, 2017 addressed to the Acting Mayor marked as Annex B)
